headline: Study suggests MSCI's ESG ratings favor stability over frequent updates
summary: A new MIT Sloan School of Management study finds that the ratings investors rely on are quietly optimized for stability by MSCI, the world’s largest ESG ratings provider, and markets miss signals that matter when changes are held back. A new MIT Sloan School of Management study finds that the ratings investors rely on are quietly optimized for stability by MSCI. content.title: Study suggests MSCI's ESG ratings favor stability over frequent updates
content.summary: A new MIT Sloan School of Management study finds that the ratings investors rely on are quietly optimized for stability by MSCI, the world’s largest ESG ratings provider, and markets miss signals that matter when changes are held back. A new MIT Sloan School of Management study finds that the ratings investors rely on are quietly optimized for stability by MSCI. Researchers found evidence that MSCI’s internal review committees often adjusted scores to keep companies from crossing ESG rating thre

thumbnail.caption: Researchers found evidence that MSCI’s internal review committees often adjusted scores to keep companies from crossing ESG rating thresholds, reducing the frequency of upgrades and downgrades even when underlying data suggested a change. Credit: iStock/VectorMine
